title: Analytic-DPM: an Analytic Estimate of the Optimal Reverse Variance in Diffusion Probabilistic Models

## ANALYTIC-DPM: AN ANALYTIC ESTIMATE OF THE - OPTIMAL REVERSE VARIANCE IN DIFFUSION PROB ABILISTIC MODELS

## **Fan Bao**[1] _[∗]_ **, Chongxuan Li**[2][3] _[†]_ **, Jun Zhu**[1] _[†]_ **, Bo Zhang**[1]

> 1Dept. of Comp. Sci. & Tech., Institute for AI, Tsinghua-Huawei Joint Center for AI BNRist Center, State Key Lab for Intell. Tech. & Sys., Tsinghua University, Beijing, China 2Gaoling School of Artificial Intelligence, Renmin University of China, Beijing, China 3Beijing Key Laboratory of Big Data Management and Analysis Methods , Beijing, China bf19@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn,chongxuanli1991@gmail.com, _{_ dcszj, dcszb _}_ @tsinghua.edu.cn

## ABSTRACT

Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) represent a class of powerful generative models. Despite their success, the inference of DPMs is expensive since it generally needs to iterate over thousands of timesteps. A key problem in the inference is to estimate the variance in each timestep of the reverse process. In this work, we present a surprising result that both the optimal reverse variance and the corresponding optimal KL divergence of a DPM have analytic forms w.r.t. its score function. Building upon it, we propose _Analytic-DPM_ , a training-free inference framework that estimates the analytic forms of the variance and KL divergence using the Monte Carlo method and a pretrained score-based model. Further, to correct the potential bias caused by the score-based model, we derive both lower and upper bounds of the optimal variance and clip the estimate for a better result. Empirically, our analytic-DPM improves the log-likelihood of various DPMs, produces high-quality samples, and meanwhile enjoys a 20 _×_ to 80 _×_ speed up.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A diffusion process gradually adds noise to a data distribution over a series of timesteps. By learning to reverse it, diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020b) define a data generative process. Recently, it is shown that DPMs are able to produce high-quality samples (Ho et al., 2020; Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021; Song et al., 2020b; Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021), which are comparable or even superior to the current state-of-the-art GAN models (Goodfellow et al., 2014; Brock et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Karras et al., 2020b).

Despite their success, the inference of DPMs (e.g., sampling and density evaluation) often requires to iterate over thousands of timesteps, which is two or three orders of magnitude slower (Song et al., 2020a) than other generative models such as GANs. A key problem in the inference is to estimate the variance in each timestep of the reverse process. Most of the prior works use a handcrafted value for all timesteps, which usually run a long chain to obtain a reasonable sample and density value (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021). Nichol & Dhariwal (2021) attempt to improve the efficiency of sampling by learning a variance network in the reverse process. However, it still needs a relatively long trajectory to get a reasonable log-likelihood (see Appendix E in Nichol & Dhariwal (2021)).

In this work, we present a surprising result that both the optimal reverse variance and the corresponding optimal KL divergence of a DPM have analytic forms w.r.t. its score function (i.e., the gradient of a log density). Building upon it, we propose _Analytic-DPM_ , a training-free inference framework to improve the efficiency of a pretrained DPM while achieving comparable or even superior performance. Analytic-DPM estimates the analytic forms of the variance and KL divergence using the Monte Carlo method and the score-based model in the pretrained DPM. The corresponding trajectory is calculated via a dynamic programming algorithm (Watson et al., 2021). Further, to

correct the potential bias caused by the score-based model, we derive both lower and upper bounds of the optimal variance and clip its estimate for a better result. Finally, we reveal an interesting relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix.

Analytic-DPM is applicable to a variety of DPMs (Ho et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020a; Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021) in a plug-and-play manner. Empirically, Analytic-DPM consistently improves the log-likelihood of these DPMs and meanwhile enjoys a 20 _×_ to 40 _×_ speed up. Besides, AnalyticDPM also consistently improves the sample quality of DDIMs (Song et al., 2020a) and requires up to 50 timesteps (which is a 20 _×_ to 80 _×_ speed up compared to the full timesteps) to achieve a comparable FID to the corresponding baseline.

The proof of Theorem 1 consists of three key steps:

- The first step (see Lemma 9) is known as the _moment matching_ (Minka, 2013), which states that approximating arbitrary density by a Gaussian density under the KL divergence is equivalent to setting the first two moments of the two densities as the same. To our knowledge, the connection of moment matching and DPMs has not been revealed before.

- In the third step (see Lemma 11), we surprisingly find that the second moment of _q_ ( _**x**_ 0 _|_ _**x** n_ ) can be represented by the score function, and we plug the score representation into the second moment of _q_ ( _**x** n−_ 1 _|_ _**x** n_ ) to get the final results in Theorem 1.

## 5 RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE SCORE FUNCTION AND THE DATA COVARIANCE MATRIX

In this part, we further reveal a relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix. Indeed, the data covariance matrix can be decomposed to the sum of E _q_ ( _**x** n_ )Cov _q_ ( _**x**_ 0 _|_ _**x** n_ )[ _**x**_ 0] and Cov _q_ ( _**x** n_ )E _q_ ( _**x**_ 0 _|_ _**x** n_ )[ _**x**_ 0], where the first term can be represented by the score function. Further, the second term is negligible when _n_ is sufficiently large because _**x**_ 0 and _**x** n_ are almost independent. In such cases, the data covariance matrix is almost determined by the score function. Currently, the relationship is purely theoretical and its practical implication is unclear. See details in Appendix A.5.

## 6 EXPERIMENTS

We consider the DDPM forward process ( _λ_[2] _n_[=] _[β]_[˜] _[n]_[)][and][the][DDIM][forward][process][(] _[λ]_[2] _n_[=][0][),] which are the two most commonly used special cases of Eq. (2). We denote our method, which uses the analytic estimate _σn_[2][=] _[σ]_[ˆ] _n_[2][,][as] _[Analytic-DPM]_[,][and][explicitly][call][it] _[Analytic-DDPM]_[or] _Analytic-DDIM_ according to which forward process is used. We compare our Analytic-DPM with the original DDPM (Ho et al., 2020), where the reverse variance is either _σn_[2][=] _[β]_[˜] _[n]_[or] _[ σ] n_[2][=] _[β][n]_[, as] well as the original DDIM (Song et al., 2020a), where the reverse variance is _σn_[2][=] _[ λ]_[2] _n_[= 0][.]

We adopt two methods to get the trajectory for both the analytic-DPM and baselines. The first one is even trajectory (ET) (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021), where the timesteps are determined according to a fixed stride (see details in Appendix F.4). The second one is optimal trajectory (OT) (Watson et al., 2021), where the timesteps are calculated via dynamic programming (see Section 4). Note that the baselines calculate the OT based on the _L_ vb with their handcrafted variances (Watson et al., 2021).

We apply our Analytic-DPM to three pretrained score-based models provided by prior works (Ho et al., 2020; Song et al., 2020a; Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021), as well as two score-based models trained by ourselves. The pretrained score-based models are trained on CelebA 64x64 (Liu et al., 2015), ImageNet 64x64 (Deng et al., 2009) and LSUN Bedroom (Yu et al., 2015) respectively. Our score-based models are trained on CIFAR10 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009) with two different forward noise schedules: the linear schedule (LS) (Ho et al., 2020) and the cosine schedule (CS) (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021). We denote them as CIFAR10 (LS) and CIFAR10 (CS) respectively. The number of the full timesteps _N_ is 4000 for ImageNet 64x64 and 1000 for other datasets. During sampling, we only display the mean of _p_ ( _**x**_ 0 _|_ _**x**_ 1) and discard the noise following Ho et al. (2020), and we additionally clip the noise scale _σ_ 2 of _p_ ( _**x**_ 1 _|_ _**x**_ 2) for all methods compared in Table 2 (see details in Appendix F.2 and its ablation study in Appendix G.4). See more experimental details in Appendix F.

## 6.1 LIKELIHOOD RESULTS

Since _λ_[2] _n_[=][0][in][the][DDIM][forward][process,][its][variational][bound] _[L]_[vb][is][infinite.][Thereby,][we] only consider the likelihood results under the DDPM forward process. As shown in Table 1, on all three datasets, our Analytic-DPM consistently improves the likelihood results of the original DDPM using both ET and OT. Remarkably, using a much shorter trajectory (i.e., a much less inference time), Analytic-DPM with OT can still outperform the baselines. In Table 1, we select the minimum _K_ such that analytic-DPM can outperform the baselines with full timesteps and underline the corresponding results. Specifically, analytic-DPM enjoys a 40 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (LS) and ImageNet 64x64, and a 20 _×_ speed up on CIFAR10 (CS) and CelebA 64x64.

Although we mainly focus on learning-free strategies of choosing the reverse variance, we also compare to another strong baseline that predicts the variance by a neural network (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021). With full timesteps, Analytic-DPM achieves a NLL of 3.61 on ImageNet 64x64, which is very close to 3.57 reported in Nichol & Dhariwal (2021). Besides, while Nichol & Dhariwal (2021) report that the ET drastically reduces the log-likelihood performance of their neural-networkparameterized variance, Analytic-DPM performs well with the ET. See details in Appendix G.6.

## 6.2 SAMPLE QUALITY

As for the sample quality, we consider the commonly used FID score (Heusel et al., 2017), where a lower value indicates a better sample quality. As shown in Table 2, under trajectories of different _K_ , our Analytic-DDIM consistently improves the sample quality of the original DDIM. This allows us to generate high-quality samples with less than 50 timesteps, which results in a 20 _×_ to 80 _×_ speed up compared to the full timesteps. Indeed, in most cases, Analytic-DDIM only requires up to 50 timesteps to get a similar performance to the baselines. Besides, Analytic-DDPM also improves the sample quality of the original DDPM in most cases. For fairness, we use the ET implementation in Nichol & Dhariwal (2021) for all results in Table 2. We also report the results on CelebA 64x64 using a slightly different implementation of the ET following Song et al. (2020a) in Appendix G.7, and our Analytic-DPM is still effective. We show generated samples in Appendix G.9.

We observe that Analytic-DDPM does not always outperform the baseline under the FID metric, which is inconsistent with the likelihood results in Table 1. Such a behavior essentially roots in the different natures of the two metrics and has been investigated in extensive prior works (Theis et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020; Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021; Song et al., 2021; Vahdat et al., 2021; Watson et al., 2021; Kingma et al., 2021). Similarly, using more timesteps doesn’t necessarily yield a better FID. For instance, see the Analytic-DDPM results on CIFAR10 (LS) and the DDIM results on ImageNet 64x64 in Table 2. A similar phenomenon is observed in Figure 8 in Nichol & Dhariwal (2021). Moreover, a DPM (including Analytic-DPM) with OT does not necessarily lead to a better FID score (Watson et al., 2021) (see Appendix G.5 for a comparison of ET and OT in Analytic-DPM).

## 7 RELATED WORK

**DPMs and their applications.** The diffusion probabilistic model (DPM) is initially introduced by Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015), where the DPM is trained by optimizing the variational bound _L_ vb. Ho et al. (2020) propose the new parameterization of DPMs in Eq. (3) and learn DPMs with the reweighted variant of _L_ vb in Eq. (5). Song et al. (2020b) model the noise adding forward process as a stochastic differential equation (SDE) and introduce DPMs with continuous timesteps. With these important improvements, DPMs show great potential in various applications, including speech synthesis (Chen et al., 2020; Kong et al., 2020; Popov et al., 2021; Lam et al., 2021), controllable generation (Choi et al., 2021; Sinha et al., 2021), image super-resolution (Saharia et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021), image-to-image translation (Sasaki et al., 2021), shape generation (Zhou et al., 2021) and time series forecasting (Rasul et al., 2021).

**Faster DPMs.** Several works attempt to find short trajectories while maintaining the DPM performance. Chen et al. (2020) find an effective trajectory of only six timesteps by the grid search. However, the grid search is only applicable to very short trajectories due to its exponentially growing time complexity. Watson et al. (2021) model the trajectory searching as a least-cost-path problem and introduce a dynamic programming (DP) algorithm to solve this problem. Our work uses this DP algorithm, where the cost is defined as a term of the optimal KL divergence. In addition to these trajectory searching techniques, Luhman & Luhman (2021) compress the reverse denoising process into a single step model; San-Roman et al. (2021) dynamically adjust the trajectory during inference. Both of them need extra training after getting a pretrained DPM. As for DPMs with continuous timesteps (Song et al., 2020b), Song et al. (2020b) introduce an ordinary differential equation (ODE), which improves sampling efficiency and enables exact likelihood computation. However, the likelihood computation involves a stochastic trace estimator, which requires a multiple number of runs for accurate computation. Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021) introduce an advanced SDE solver to simulate the reverse process in a more efficient way. However, the log-likelihood computation based on this solver is not specified.

**Variance Learning in DPMs.** In addition to the reverse variance, there are also works on learning the forward noise schedule (i.e., the forward variance). Kingma et al. (2021) propose variational diffusion models (VDMs) on continuous timesteps, which use a signal-to-noise ratio function to parameterize the forward variance and directly optimize the variational bound objective for a better log-likelihood. While we primarily apply our method to DDPMs and DDIMs, estimating the optimal reverse variance can also be applied to VDMs (see Appendix E).

## 8 CONCLUSION

We present that both the optimal reverse variance and the corresponding optimal KL divergence of a DPM have analytic forms w.r.t. its score function. Building upon it, we propose _AnalyticDPM_ , a training-free inference framework that estimates the analytic forms of the variance and KL divergence using the Monte Carlo method and a pretrained score-based model. We derive bounds of the optimal variance to correct potential bias and reveal a relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix. Empirically, our analytic-DPM improves both the efficiency and performance of likelihood results, and generates high-quality samples efficiently in various DPMs.

As a fundamental research in machine learning, the negative consequences are not obvious. Though in theory any technique can be misused, it is not likely to happen at the current stage.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
Analytic-DPM proposes a training-free method to estimate the optimal reverse variance in diffusion models using an analytic form derived from the score function. By leveraging Monte Carlo estimation of the score norm and applying dynamic programming for trajectory optimization, the authors demonstrate significant speedups in sampling and improvements in log-likelihood.

The paper provides a elegant theoretical derivation connecting reverse variance to the score function, which is a significant contribution to the field of diffusion models. The methodology is well-motivated and the empirical results convincingly demonstrate the efficiency gains. It is a high-quality paper that addresses a practical bottleneck in diffusion inference.

Overview issues:

- Sensitivity of Variance Estimate to Score Approximation Error: The analytic variance estimate in Equation (9) relies directly on the norm of the score function, which is approximated by a neural network. As noted in Section 3.1, the approximation error in the score function propagates into the variance estimate. While the authors propose clipping to mitigate this, there is no formal analysis of how the variance of the score estimator itself (due to finite Monte Carlo samples or neural network training noise) affects the stability of the reverse process. A more rigorous treatment would characterize the sensitivity of the reverse trajectory to these estimation errors, particularly in low-data regimes or at extreme noise levels where the score function is notoriously difficult to learn.

- Gaussian Assumption for Reverse Transitions: Theorem 1 and the subsequent derivations assume that the reverse transition $p(x_{n-1}|x_n)$ is Gaussian. While this is standard practice in DDPMs, the paper does not address cases where the true reverse distribution might be highly non-Gaussian, especially for complex data manifolds or when using large step sizes. The reliance on moment matching (Lemma 9) effectively forces a Gaussian approximation, which may be suboptimal if the true posterior $q(x_{n-1}|x_n, x_0)$ is multi-modal or skewed. The authors should clarify the conditions under which this Gaussian assumption holds and discuss potential failure modes when the score function is poorly calibrated for non-Gaussian densities.

- Computational Overhead of Monte Carlo Estimation: The paper claims the method is training-free, yet it requires computing $\Gamma_n$ (Equation 8) through Monte Carlo sampling across all timesteps. While the authors state this is done once, they do not provide a detailed breakdown of the computational cost of this pre-inference step compared to the actual sampling speedup. If the number of samples $M$ required for a stable estimate is large, this could offset the efficiency gains for applications where only a few samples are generated. A more transparent analysis of the total cost (pre-computation plus inference) would strengthen the claim of improved efficiency.

- Discretization Error and Theoretical Bounds: The derivation of the optimal variance (Theorem 1) and the trajectory optimization (Section 4) are based on the discrete-time formulation. However, the paper ignores the discretization error inherent in the reverse SDE/ODE process, which often dominates the sampling error when $K$ is small. The bounds provided in Theorem 2 are independent of the data distribution, which is clever, but they do not account for the divergence between the discrete-time model and the underlying continuous-time SDE. A discussion on how the optimal variance shifts as the discretization step size changes would provide a more complete theoretical picture.

- Missing empirical validation of the score-covariance relationship: Section 5 claims a theoretical relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix but explicitly states that the practical implications are unclear. Given the paper's emphasis on analytic forms, it would be highly beneficial to compute this relationship for a simple, low-dimensional Gaussian mixture model where the true covariance and score function are known analytically. This would demonstrate whether the derived relationship serves as a useful diagnostic for the score-based model's calibration or if it is merely a mathematical curiosity. Providing a simple numerical verification of this identity would ground the paper's theoretical contributions in a concrete, verifiable setting.

- Absence of a sensitivity analysis for the clipping bounds: While Theorem 2 provides upper and lower bounds for the optimal variance to mitigate score estimation bias, the paper does not demonstrate how the choice of these bounds affects the final sample quality or log-likelihood. It is unclear if the clipping is overly conservative or if it masks significant errors in the score function approximation. A simulation study using a synthetic dataset, such as a 2D mixture of Gaussians, where the optimal variance can be computed exactly, would show how the clipped Analytic-DPM estimate compares to the theoretical optimum. This would provide the reader with a clear understanding of the 'bite' of the proposed bounds in a controlled environment.

Recommendation: Accept. The paper provides a clear, theoretically grounded, and empirically validated improvement to diffusion model inference. The limitations are acknowledged, and the proposed solutions (clipping, dynamic programming) are practical and effective.

Revision targets:

- Include a sensitivity analysis showing how errors in the score function norm estimation propagate into the variance estimate and subsequent sample quality.

- Provide a explicit comparison of the total computational budget (Monte Carlo pre-computation + sampling) versus standard baselines to clarify the 'training-free' efficiency claims.

- Discuss the impact of the Gaussian assumption on the reverse transition when the score function is applied to highly non-Gaussian data manifolds.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding bounds and covariance relationship
comments.quote: We derive bounds of the optimal variance to correct potential bias and reveal a relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims the paper derives bounds for the optimal variance and reveals a relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix. However, the formal results in Section 3 focus on an analytic representation of the optimal variance (Theorem 1) and its Monte Carlo estimation (Eq. 9). There is no theorem or lemma presented that explicitly derives bounds for the optimal variance to correct bias, nor is there a formal derivation linking the score function to the data covariance matrix. Please remove these claims from the conclusion or provide the specific formal results that establish these properties.

comments.title: Unjustified link between posterior covariance and score function
comments.quote: Indeed, the data covariance matrix can be decomposed to the sum of $\mathbb{E}_{q(\bm{x}_{n})}\mathrm{Cov}_{q(\bm{x}_{0}|\bm{x}_{n})}[\bm{x}_{0}]$ and $\mathrm{Cov}_{q(\bm{x}_{n})}\mathbb{E}_{q(\bm{x}_{0}|\bm{x}_{n})}[\bm{x}_{0}]$, where the first term can be represented by the score function.
comments.explanation: While the Law of Total Covariance is correctly stated, the claim that the first term (the expected posterior covariance) can be represented by the score function is not supported. Tweedie's formula relates the posterior mean to the score, but the posterior covariance generally depends on the Hessian of the log-density or higher-order moments. Unless you assume a specific distribution like a Gaussian, the posterior covariance is not a function of the score alone. Please provide a derivation for this claim or remove the assertion that the first term is represented by the score function.

comments.title: Potential notation conflict in variance expression
comments.quote: \sigma_{n}^{*2}=\lambda_{n}^{2}+\left(\sqrt{\frac{\overline{\beta}_{n}}{\alpha_{n}}}-\sqrt{\overline{\beta}_{n-1}-\lambda_{n}^{2}}\right)^{2}\left(1-\overline{\beta}_{n}\mathbb{E}_{q_{n}(\bm{x}_{n})}\frac{||\nabla_{\bm{x}_{n}}\log q_{n}(\bm{x}_{n})||^{2}}{d}\right)
comments.explanation: There appears to be a dimensional inconsistency in the expression for $\sigma_n^{*2}$. In standard DDPM notation, $\alpha_n$ is the step-wise parameter $1-\beta_n$, while $\bar{\beta}_n$ is a cumulative variance parameter. Using $\alpha_n$ in the denominator of the square root term while using cumulative $\bar{\beta}$ terms elsewhere suggests a possible typo. It is likely that $\bar{\alpha}_n$ was intended to maintain dimensional consistency. Please verify this and update the manuscript accordingly.

comments.title: Inconsistency in speedup claims
comments.quote: ner. Empirically, Analytic-DPM consistently improves the log-likelihood of these DPMs and meanwhile enjoys a $20\times$ to $40\times$ speed up. Besides, Analytic-DPM also consistently improves the sample quality of DDIMs *(Song et al., 2020a)* and requires up to 50 timesteps (which is a $20\times$ to $80\times$ speed up compared to the full timesteps)
comments.explanation: The introduction provides two different ranges for the speedup (20x-40x vs 20x-80x) without clarifying the distinction between the metrics (log-likelihood vs FID) or the experimental conditions. This creates ambiguity regarding the efficiency gains. Please clarify which speedup range corresponds to which evaluation metric or experimental setup.

comments.title: Inconsistency in the definition of the cost function J
comments.quote: where $J(\tau_{k-1},\tau_{k})=\log(\sigma_{\tau_{k-1}^{1}|\tau_{k}}^{2}/\lambda_{\tau_{k-1}|\tau_{k}}^{2})$ and $c$ is a constant unrelated to the trajectory $\tau$
comments.explanation: The superscript '1' in the subscript $\tau_{k-1}^{1}$ appears to be a typographical error, as it is inconsistent with the notation $\sigma_{\tau_{k-1}|\tau_{k}}^{*2}$ introduced earlier. Please rewrite this as $J(\tau_{k-1},\tau_{k})=\log(\sigma_{\tau_{k-1}|\tau_{k}}^{*2}/\lambda_{\tau_{k-1}|\tau_{k}}^{2})$ to maintain consistency.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'training-free' inference framework claim
comments.quote: Building upon it, we propose Analytic-DPM, a training-free inference framework that estimates the analytic forms of the variance and KL divergence using the Monte Carlo method and a pretrained score-based model.
comments.explanation: The term 'training-free' is potentially misleading because the framework relies on a pretrained score-based model, which is the result of extensive training. Please clarify that the method avoids re-training the score model, rather than implying the entire pipeline is devoid of training requirements. A phrasing such as 'a training-free inference framework that leverages a pretrained score-based model without further model training' would be more accurate.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Analytic-DPM, a training-free inference framework that estimates the optimal reverse variance in diffusion models using the score function and Monte Carlo sampling. The authors derive analytic forms for the optimal variance and KL divergence, introduce bounding strategies to clip estimates, and use dynamic programming to optimize sampling trajectories. The work addresses a practical bottleneck in diffusion sampling by reducing reliance on handcrafted variance schedules and long trajectories.

The manuscript tackles a relevant problem in diffusion probabilistic models by offering a closed-form expression for the optimal reverse variance and demonstrating empirical gains in likelihood and sampling efficiency. The theoretical derivation is straightforward, and the dynamic programming trajectory optimization integrates cleanly with existing frameworks. The main strength lies in the plug-and-play nature of the method and the consistent likelihood improvements across multiple datasets.

Overview issues:

- Monte Carlo Estimator Variance and Preprocessing Overhead: The method relies on estimating the expected squared score norm via Monte Carlo sampling from $q_n(x_n)$ at every timestep (Eq. 8). The paper claims that only $M=10$ or $100$ samples suffice, but it never reports the variance of this estimator or how it scales with data dimensionality. In high-dimensional image spaces, the squared norm of the score concentrates sharply, yet small estimation errors can shift the variance estimate outside the theoretically derived bounds. More importantly, the preprocessing cost of drawing $M$ samples per timestep for $N=1000$ steps is substantial and directly contradicts the claimed $20	imes$ to $80	imes$ speedup if wall-clock time is considered. The authors should quantify the one-time MC cost, include it in the efficiency comparison, and provide a sensitivity analysis showing how estimator variance affects downstream NLL and FID scores before claiming the method is computationally lightweight.

- Clipping Strategy Introduces Unquantified Bias: Section 3.1 introduces clipping based on the bounds in Theorem 2 to mitigate score model bias. While clipping prevents pathological variance values, it arbitrarily truncates the MC estimate without analyzing how this modification affects the variational bound $L_{\mathrm{vb}}$. The clipping operation effectively replaces the theoretically optimal variance with a suboptimal one whenever the MC estimate falls outside $[\lambda_n^2, \text{bound}]$. The paper does not ablate the raw MC estimate against the clipped version to demonstrate that clipping actually improves the objective rather than merely stabilizing it. A rigorous analysis should show how the clipping threshold interacts with the score estimation error in Eq. 10, and the authors must provide an ablation comparing unclipped versus clipped variance schedules to justify the design choice.

- Trajectory Optimization Under Stochastic Cost Estimates: The dynamic programming algorithm in Section 4 minimizes the sum of KL terms using the cost function $J(\tau_{k-1}, \tau_k) = \log(\hat{\sigma}^2/\lambda^2)$ (Eq. 14). The DP formulation assumes a fixed, deterministic cost matrix, yet the costs here are derived from noisy Monte Carlo estimates that vary across runs. The paper acknowledges that the logarithmic function introduces bias but offers no error propagation analysis or robustness guarantee for the DP solution. If the cost matrix fluctuates, the least-cost path may oscillate or converge to a suboptimal trajectory. The authors should either provide a stochastic DP variant, report the variance of the selected trajectories across multiple MC seeds, or demonstrate that the estimated cost matrix is sufficiently stable for the DP algorithm to yield consistent results.

- Empirical Isolation and Metric Discrepancies: Table 1 shows consistent NLL improvements, while Table 2 reveals cases where Analytic-DDPM or Analytic-DDIM underperforms the baseline in FID (e.g., CIFAR10 LS at $K=1000$). The authors attribute this to metric differences but do not isolate the variance estimation effect from trajectory choice or other hyperparameters. The even trajectory implementation follows Nichol & Dhariwal (2021), yet the baseline DDPM results in Table 2 already use an improved architecture, making direct comparisons with the original Ho et al. (2020) setup ambiguous. The paper should provide an ablation that holds the trajectory fixed while swapping only the variance schedule, and it must clarify whether the reported FID improvements stem from the analytic variance or from the trajectory optimization. Without this isolation, the claim that the variance estimate alone drives sample quality gains remains unsupported.

- Theoretical Framing Overstates Novelty: The authors describe the analytic form of the optimal variance as a surprising result and claim that the connection between moment matching and DPMs has not been revealed before (Section 3). This characterization misrepresents the derivation. The result follows directly from applying Gaussian moment matching to the variational bound in Eq. 4, a standard technique in variational inference that has been used in related works like Variational Diffusion Models (Kingma et al., 2021). The true contribution lies in the practical estimation pipeline and the bounding strategy, not in a new theoretical identity. The framing should be tempered to accurately position the work within the existing variational inference literature, and the novelty should be explicitly tied to the training-free variance estimation and trajectory optimization rather than the analytic form itself.

- Robustness to Imperfect Score Estimates Lacks Discussion: Equation 10 shows that the estimation error depends directly on the gap between the learned score model and the true score. The paper acknowledges score bias in the literature context but provides no analysis or experiments on how performance degrades when the score model is intentionally degraded or evaluated on regions with high estimation uncertainty. Diffusion models are known to struggle with score estimation in low-density or multimodal regions, and the analytic variance estimate will inherit these failures. The authors should include a failure case analysis, such as testing on a dataset with known score estimation challenges or perturbing the score model to simulate estimation noise, to clarify the operational boundaries of the method. Without this, the claim of consistent improvement across diverse DPMs lacks empirical grounding.

- Worked parametric example to calibrate the analytic variance: The paper derives general analytic forms for the optimal variance but never computes them for a fully specified parametric model. Readers cannot easily gauge when the analytic estimate significantly deviates from handcrafted schedules like $\beta_n$ or $\hat{\beta}_n$. The authors should compute Equation (7) for a simple one-dimensional Gaussian or a two-component Gaussian mixture. This worked example would demonstrate exactly how the score-dependent term shifts the variance schedule and verify that the derived bounds hold in a tractable setting. Without it, the theoretical result remains abstract and disconnected from the data geometry.

- Underdeveloped implications of the score-covariance relationship: Section 5 claims a relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix as a key finding, yet explicitly states that its practical implication is unclear. The abstract and introduction list this relationship among the main contributions, so leaving it as a theoretical dead end creates a gap between the paper's promises and its delivery. The authors should either develop a concrete downstream application for this relationship—such as using it to initialize the score network or to construct a fast likelihood estimator—or remove it from the contribution list. As written, it reads like an unexplored lemma rather than a completed result.

- Missing evaluation against deterministic few-step samplers: The calibration notes compatibility with fast samplers like DPM-Solver, but the experiments only compare against DDPM and DDIM using even or dynamic trajectories. Modern few-step generation heavily relies on deterministic ODE solvers, which bypass stochastic variance entirely. The paper does not discuss how the analytic variance estimate integrates with these solvers or report results using them. Adding a comparison to DPM-Solver or a standard Euler-Maruyama ODE sampler would clarify whether the analytic variance provides gains beyond what deterministic discretization already achieves. Without this, the speedup claims lack context against the current sampling standard.

- Implementation guidance for Monte Carlo estimator scaling: The entire training-free framework hinges on estimating the expected squared score norm via Monte Carlo sampling at each timestep. The paper asserts that $M=10$ or $100$ samples suffice but provides no theoretical or empirical guidance on how this sample size should scale with the data dimensionality $d$. In high-dimensional image spaces, the squared norm of the score concentrates sharply, and small estimation errors can push the variance estimate outside the derived bounds. The authors should discuss the convergence rate of this estimator with respect to $d$, provide a sensitivity analysis showing how $M$ affects downstream NLL and FID across different resolutions, and clarify the computational trade-offs of increasing $M$.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Quantify the Monte Carlo preprocessing cost and include it in the speedup metric; add a variance analysis for the estimator in Eq. 8 and report stability across multiple MC seeds.

- Provide an ablation isolating the variance schedule from the trajectory optimization, and explicitly compare raw MC estimates against the clipped version to justify the bounding strategy.

- Analyze how stochastic cost estimates affect the dynamic programming trajectory selection in Section 4, either by reporting trajectory variance or adopting a robust optimization variant.

- Reframe the theoretical contribution to accurately reflect the variational moment-matching derivation, and clearly separate the novel estimation pipeline from standard variational inference results.

- Add a robustness analysis demonstrating performance under degraded score estimates or on datasets with known score estimation challenges to clarify the method's operational limits.

comments.title: Missing multiplicative factor in Theorem 2 upper bound
comments.quote: $\lambda_{n}^{2}\leq\sigma_{n}^{*2}\leq\lambda_{n}^{2}+\left(\sqrt{\frac{\bar{\beta}_{n}}{\alpha_{n}}}-\sqrt{\bar{\beta}_{n-1}-\lambda_{n}^{2}}\right)^{2}.$ (11)
comments.explanation: Equation (11) omits a multiplicative factor of $\bar{\beta}_n$ that appears explicitly in the coefficient of Equation (10). The bias expression in Eq. (10) defines the coefficient as $C_n = \left(\sqrt{\frac{\bar{\beta}_n}{\alpha_n}} - \sqrt{\bar{\beta}_{n-1} - \lambda_n^2}\right)^2 \bar{\beta}_n$. If the upper bound in Theorem 2 is derived by bounding this coefficient, the $\bar{\beta}_n$ term must carry through. Its absence creates an algebraic inconsistency between the bias analysis and the stated bounds. The authors should verify the derivation in Appendix A.3 and correct Eq. (11) to include $\bar{\beta}_n$, or explain why the factor drops out during the bounding step.

comments.title: Incorrect KL decomposition omits variance ratio term
comments.explanation: The exact KL divergence between two $d$-dimensional Gaussians with matched means and variances $\lambda^2$ and $\sigma^{*2}$ is $\frac{d}{2}\left(\frac{\lambda^2}{\sigma^{*2}} - 1 + \log\frac{\sigma^{*2}}{\lambda^2}\right)$. The paper's cost function $J$ only captures the logarithmic term, implicitly assuming the $\frac{\lambda^2}{\sigma^{*2}} - 1$ component is constant across trajectories. However, both $\lambda^2$ and $\sigma^{*2}$ depend on the specific timestep indices $\tau_{k-1}$ and $\tau_k$, so their ratio varies with the trajectory choice. Consequently, minimizing $J$ does not minimize the true KL divergence. The authors should either redefine the edge cost to include the full KL expression, or explicitly state that $J$ is an approximation used for trajectory selection rather than an exact decomposition. Additionally, the superscript '1' in $\sigma_{\tau_{k-1}^{1}|\tau_{k}}^{2}$ is a typographical error for $*$.

comments.title: Variance formula requires constraint on $\lambda_n^2$ for real values
comments.quote: $\sigma_{n}^{*2}=\lambda_{n}^{2}+\left(\sqrt{\frac{\overline{\beta}_{n}}{\alpha_{n}}}-\sqrt{\overline{\beta}_{n-1}-\lambda_{n}^{2}}\right)^{2}\left(1-\overline{\beta}_{n}\mathbb{E}_{q_{n}(\bm{x}_{n})}\frac{||\nabla_{\bm{x}_{n}}\log q_{n}(\bm{x}_{n})||^{2}}{d}\right),$ (7)
comments.explanation: Equation (7) contains the term $\sqrt{\overline{\beta}_{n-1}-\lambda_{n}^{2}}$. For the analytic variance estimate to remain real-valued, the argument of the square root must be non-negative, requiring $\lambda_n^2 \le \overline{\beta}_{n-1}$. The theorem statement and surrounding text do not impose this constraint. If $\lambda_n^2$ is selected as a hyperparameter without respecting this bound, the formula yields complex numbers, breaking the variance estimation. Add the condition $\lambda_n^2 \le \overline{\beta}_{n-1}$ to the assumptions of Theorem 1 and clarify how this constraint guides the choice of $\lambda_n^2$ in practice.

comments.title: Score function alone cannot determine posterior second moments
comments.quote: In the third step (see Lemma 11), we surprisingly find that the second moment of $q(\bm{x}_{0}|\bm{x}_{n})$ can be represented by the score function, and we plug the score representation into the second moment of $q(\bm{x}_{n-1}|\bm{x}_{n})$ to get the final results in Theorem 1.
comments.explanation:
The claim that the second moment of $q( m{x}_0| m{x}_n)$ is determined solely by the score function $
abla_{ m{x}_n}\log q_n( m{x}_n)$ is mathematically imprecise for arbitrary distributions. The score function uniquely identifies the first moment via $\mathbb{E}[ m{x}_0| m{x}_n] = \sqrt{\overline{\alpha}_n} m{x}_n + \overline{ eta}_n 
abla_{ m{x}_n}\log q_n( m{x}_n)$. Recovering the second moment $\mathbb{E}[ m{x}_0 m{x}_0^T| m{x}_n]$ requires the conditional covariance $	ext{Cov}( m{x}_0| m{x}_n)$. By the Gaussian perturbation identity, $	ext{Cov}( m{x}_0| m{x}_n) = \overline{ eta}_n  m{I} + \overline{ eta}_n^2 
abla^2_{ m{x}_n}\log q_n( m{x}_n)$, meaning the second moment depends on the Hessian of the score, not the score itself. Alternatively, the derivation implicitly assumes a specific prior (e.g., $ m{x}_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,  m{I})$) which fixes the covariance to $\overline{ eta}_n  m{I}$. The proof should explicitly state the required prior assumption or clarify that the Hessian of the score is used to recover the second moment.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about optimal KL divergence
comments.quote: We present that both the optimal reverse variance and the corresponding optimal KL divergence of a DPM have analytic forms w.r.t. its score function.
comments.explanation: The formal results section establishes analytic forms only for the optimal reverse mean and variance (Theorem 1, Eqs. 6–7). The provided text contains no theorem, derivation, or equation demonstrating that the optimal KL divergence admits an analytic form with respect to the score function. The abstract and introduction overstate the scope of the formal results by including the KL divergence. The authors should either provide the missing derivation for the optimal KL divergence or remove it from the list of analytic forms to accurately reflect the paper's theoretical contributions.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a clean theoretical result—analytic forms for the optimal reverse variance and KL divergence in DPMs—and demonstrates consistent likelihood improvements with a training-free Monte Carlo plug-in. The derivation is rigorous, the bounds are well-motivated, and the empirical validation covers multiple datasets and sampling budgets. The contribution is significant for the diffusion modeling community, as it replaces ad hoc variance choices with a principled, computationally cheap alternative.

Overview issues:

- Assumption that the score-based model is a perfect proxy for the true score function: The entire analytic estimate in Eq. (9) replaces the true score ∇ log q_n with the learned model s_n. The bias analysis in Eq. (10) acknowledges this approximation error but treats it as an irreducible given. In practice, the pretrained score model may have substantial errors, especially in low-density regions or at early timesteps where the data distribution is highly multimodal. The paper does not characterize how sensitive the variance estimate is to score model quality (e.g., varying training epochs, architectures, or noise schedules). A natural question is whether the bounds in Theorem 2 are tight enough to compensate for a poor score model. The authors should include an ablation that degrades the score model (e.g., by using an under-trained checkpoint) and reports the resulting likelihood and FID.

- Discretization gap between continuous-time derivation and discrete-time sampling: Theorem 1 is derived for the discrete-time forward process in Eq. (2), but the paper claims in Section 3 that the result extends to continuous-time DPMs (Appendix E.1). However, the discrete-time sampling procedure in Section 4 uses a shorter trajectory of K steps, which is a coarse discretization of the continuous-time SDE. The optimal variance formula in Eq. (7) depends on the specific discretization (the λ_n parameters). When using a sub-sampled trajectory, the effective step size changes, and the formula in Eq. (14) assumes the same functional form as the full-step case. The paper does not analyze the discretization error introduced by this sub-sampling. A rigorous treatment would either derive a continuous-time optimal variance and then discretize, or provide a bound on the KL degradation due to discretization. Without this, the claim that the analytic estimate is optimal for the shorter trajectory is unsubstantiated.

- Unsupported claim that the optimal variance is independent of the data distribution beyond the score norm: Equation (7) expresses σ_n^{*2} solely in terms of λ_n, the noise schedule parameters, and the expected squared norm of the score. This suggests that the optimal variance does not depend on higher-order moments or the covariance structure of the data distribution. While the derivation in Lemma 11 and Lemma 13 uses the law of total variance, it implicitly assumes that the conditional distribution q(x_0 | x_n) is Gaussian (or that its second moment is captured by the score). For non-Gaussian data (e.g., natural images with discrete pixel values or multi-modal distributions), the second moment of q(x_0 | x_n) may not be fully determined by the score. The paper should explicitly state the conditions under which the score representation of the second moment holds (e.g., when the data distribution is a mixture of Gaussians or when the forward process is linear Gaussian). The empirical success on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet suggests the approximation works in practice, but the theoretical gap should be acknowledged.

- Missing empirical validation of the relationship between score function and data covariance: Section 5 claims a relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix, but the paper states that 'its practical implication is unclear' and provides no experiments. For a paper that emphasizes theoretical contributions, this section feels incomplete. The relationship could be tested by computing the score-based estimate of the covariance (via the decomposition in Appendix A.5) and comparing it to the empirical covariance of the data. This would provide a sanity check on the theoretical framework and might reveal limitations (e.g., when the second term is not negligible). The authors should either remove this section or include a simple empirical demonstration (e.g., on synthetic data or a small image dataset) showing that the score-based covariance estimate is accurate.

- Inconsistent FID results and lack of statistical significance: Table 2 shows that Analytic-DDPM sometimes underperforms the baseline DDPM with σ_n^2 = β_n on FID (e.g., on CIFAR-10 (LS) at K=1000: 4.03 vs. 3.04). The paper attributes this to the different natures of likelihood and FID, but this is a critical limitation: the proposed method can worsen sample quality even as it improves likelihood. The paper does not report confidence intervals or multiple runs for FID, making it impossible to assess whether the differences are statistically significant. Given that FID is a standard metric for generative models, the paper should include error bars (e.g., over 5 runs) and discuss the trade-off between likelihood and FID more concretely. The claim that Analytic-DPM 'produces high-quality samples' is weakened by these inconsistent results.

- Limited discussion of computational overhead for Monte Carlo estimation: The paper states that only M=10 or 100 Monte Carlo samples are needed (Appendix G.2), but the cost of generating these samples is not fully accounted for. Each sample requires a forward pass through the score model at each timestep n to compute ||s_n(x_n)||^2. For N=1000 timesteps and M=100 samples, this is 100,000 score model evaluations—a non-trivial one-time cost. The paper mentions that Γ is computed once and reused, but does not report the wall-clock time or GPU-hours for this precomputation. For practitioners, this overhead may be a barrier to adoption. The authors should provide a clear cost breakdown (e.g., time to compute Γ vs. time saved by using fewer sampling steps) and discuss scenarios where the overhead is justified.

- Assumption that the optimal reverse variance is isotropic (scalar) is not justified: Throughout the paper, the reverse variance is assumed to be isotropic: σ_n^2 I. Theorem 1 derives the optimal scalar variance, but the true optimal reverse covariance might be a full matrix (or at least diagonal with different entries per dimension). The moment-matching lemma (Lemma 9) states that the optimal Gaussian approximation matches the first two moments of the target distribution. If the target covariance is not isotropic, the optimal reverse covariance should be the full conditional covariance of q(x_{n-1} | x_n). The paper's restriction to isotropic variance is a modeling choice that limits optimality. The authors should justify why an isotropic approximation is sufficient (e.g., by arguing that the forward process noise is isotropic and the score model is well-conditioned) or acknowledge that the derived variance is optimal only within the isotropic family.

- No worked parametric example demonstrating the analytic formulas: The paper derives analytic forms for the optimal reverse variance and KL divergence in Theorem 1, but never computes these quantities for a simple, fully-specified data distribution. For instance, if the data distribution is a single Gaussian or a mixture of two Gaussians, one can analytically compute the score function and the expected squared score norm, then plug them into Eq. (7) to obtain a closed-form expression for σ_n^{*2}. This would let readers verify that the formula is correct, see how the variance depends on the noise schedule and data covariance, and check that the bounds in Theorem 2 are tight. Without such a concrete example, the theoretical results remain abstract and their correctness is harder to assess. The paper should include a short section (or appendix) that computes Eq. (7) for a Gaussian data distribution with known covariance and compares it to the handcrafted variances β_n and β̂_n.

- Missing demonstration that the variance estimate is non-vacuous: The paper claims that the handcrafted variances (β_n, β̂_n) are suboptimal, but it never constructs a data-generating process where the optimal variance differs substantially from these baselines and where the analytic estimate recovers the correct value. For example, one could take a simple two-component Gaussian mixture as the data distribution, compute the true score and the true optimal variance via Eq. (7), and then show that β_n and β̂_n give a different value. This would demonstrate that the analytic estimate has 'bite'—it is not trivially equal to the handcrafted values. The paper should include such a synthetic experiment, plotting the true optimal variance, the analytic estimate (using the true score), and the baselines across timesteps, and reporting the resulting KL divergence.

- No guidance on estimating the score norm expectation in practice: The Monte Carlo estimate Γ_n in Eq. (8) requires sampling from q_n(x_n) and evaluating the score model. The paper does not discuss how to sample from q_n(x_n) efficiently: one must either draw x_0 from the training set and add noise, or use ancestral sampling from the forward process. The cost of this precomputation (number of score model evaluations) is mentioned only briefly in Appendix G.2, but the paper does not report actual wall-clock time or GPU-hours for any dataset. Without this information, a practitioner cannot assess whether the one-time cost is justified by the sampling speed-up. The paper should include a table showing the time to compute Γ for each dataset (e.g., CIFAR-10, CelebA) and the total sampling time saved when using Analytic-DPM with K=50 steps versus the baseline with K=1000 steps.

- Underdeveloped implications of the score-covariance relationship: Section 5 claims a relationship between the score function and the data covariance matrix, but explicitly states that 'its practical implication is unclear.' This section is presented as a contribution in the abstract and introduction, yet the paper provides no empirical validation or application. For a paper that emphasizes theoretical contributions, this feels incomplete. The authors should either remove this claim from the contributions or provide a simple experiment—for example, on synthetic data or a small image dataset—showing that the score-based estimate of the data covariance (via the decomposition in Appendix A.5) matches the empirical covariance. This would demonstrate that the relationship is not merely formal and could have future uses, such as in covariance estimation or dimensionality reduction.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add an ablation study varying the quality of the pretrained score model (e.g., using an under-trained checkpoint) to show how sensitive the variance estimate and downstream metrics are to score model accuracy.

- Provide a theoretical or empirical analysis of the discretization error when using the analytic variance formula on a sub-sampled trajectory of K steps, including a bound on the KL degradation relative to the full-step optimal.

- Include confidence intervals or multiple-run statistics for all FID and likelihood results in Tables 1 and 2 to establish statistical significance.

- Report the computational cost of computing Γ (wall-clock time, GPU-hours) and compare it to the time saved by using fewer sampling steps, to help practitioners assess the trade-off.

- Explicitly state the conditions under which the score representation of the second moment of q(x_0|x_n) holds (e.g., Gaussian mixture data), and discuss the limitations of the isotropic variance assumption.

comments.title: Optimal variance claim requires explicit isotropy assumption
comments.quote: In this work, we present a surprising result that both the optimal reverse variance and the corresponding optimal KL divergence of a DPM have analytic forms w.r.t. its score function.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the optimal reverse variance has an analytic form, but the derivation in Theorem 1 assumes the reverse variance is isotropic (scalar). The true optimal reverse covariance for a Gaussian approximation should match the full conditional covariance of q(x_{n-1}|x_n) via moment matching (Lemma 9). Restricting to isotropic variance is a modeling choice that limits optimality; the paper does not justify why this restriction is sufficient. The claim should explicitly state that the result gives the optimal isotropic variance, not the optimal full covariance. Rewrite the abstract to say 'the optimal isotropic reverse variance' to avoid overclaiming.
